speaker: interviewer
text: --tape two. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Irene, you were telling me that Poland and Germany and Russia made a pact. And they divided your country, Poland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you wound up on the Russian side.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's correct. I was on the Russian side. And we were trying to find the food to barter with the villagers. And of a second, the Russians truck camp. Somebody must say that there is the soldiers or something in the forest. And I was so scared that I ran. Three Russian soldiers got ahold of me. I was fighting, yelling, screaming. They raped me. And they beat me up. And they left me now to die.

speaker: interviewer
text: They raped you in the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the woods, yes, because I ran. That was my home. I was so scared. They were with the bayonets and things. And I was just scared.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you, would you have not run, if you gave up, what would have happened, do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I was scared. I run. I know. Maybe if I would have kept up with all the others-- see, I was only alone standing because the other were in the houses, bartering. So maybe I would be with the others, it would not happen. But I ran, and I was captured by the Russian. And then they picked up all the people from the village-- the soldiers and the other nurse. She was older. And when they were taking them, and they were passing by, they did see me laying on the floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Polish soldiers were making a noise, and saying, you come. You're supposedly helping, trying to help us. Look, the women-- and they picked me up, threw me on a truck.

speaker: interviewee
text: Next thing, the motion of the truck, I came to it. And I realized that I was covered with the coat, military coat. And I did see-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] realize what was happening. I was ashamed. And I was scared. And I was cold. So because I was hurt, they dropped me in a Russian hospital. And I never did see anymore the other soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those were the Polish--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Polish. They were, like, prisoners of war-- taken. And I was put in a hospital because I was hurt. And I remember I was half-naked, half-frozen. I remember my-- I could not see out of my eyes because I guess I was fighting. They kicked me in the face. I thought, I am blind. I remember screaming, Mama, Mama, Mama. And of a second, I felt two warm arms wrap around my shoulder. A woman's voice was speaking to me, but with a language that I could not understand. But the voice alone-- that there was somebody, I started crying, crying. She was holding me. And she was saying, [RUSSIAN]. I did not understand, but it was soothing to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: I love you. You're like my daughter. It was a woman doctor. And she was very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] warm to me now. She took care of me. I was a little girl. I was in her hands. And she did clean me up, get me medical help, and also help like mother would do in this situation. And when I-- that was now January, end of January, 1940. And when I first met her, she gave me a job to tend in a hospital. She knew I was a student, so she gave me-- I worked 12 hours-- and then 24 hours, and then 12 hours rest.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a hospital was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a hospital for wounded. And there were Polish-- there were Russian and Polish. And also, part of the-- there was an outbreak of cholera. And that was in another part of the hospital did have a-- through the garden, there was-- I knew that-- they told me that there is a part that you cannot go there in, that it's not good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a quarantine?

speaker: interviewee
text: A quarantine, yes. And so I was-- she liked me, the woman doctor. And I lived with two Russian nurses. We lived-- we could line the room. It was one room after other. And we could not-- And I know that I come after 24-hours job. And I was very thirsty. I brought a bottle of tea in a bottle. The rooms we did not-- I did not have anything. You know, I didn't have a hospital nightgown. But underneath the bed to put whatever I did have. And the two Russian nurses, they did have dresses. There was a Marushka, Russian nurse, very fine. She shared with me some panties and, you know, helped me. The other nurse was big-built, big-breasted, and a hellion. She could drink vodka from the bathroom, you know, really unbelievable. And one day, the nurse-- I tried to learn Russian as soon as I can because in the hospital working. And one day, the doctor invited me after the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 24 hours, she said, you're a very good nurse. You work hard. I would like you to come and have a dinner with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry, before you go to dinner with her, what was the names of the two nurses that you were working with?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- one was [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] Helga. And the other one is Miriam-- Miriam or Marianne. I did know, and just flew out of my head, the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know from where?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They just put me together with them to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the doctor that invited you to dinner, was she--

speaker: interviewee
text: Olga Pavlovsky, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --also Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Was a Russian woman doctor in maybe late 40s, early 50s. And she invited-- the dinner was wonderful, a first time to really take a nice, hot bath. And she did have a sink for me to wash my hair.

speaker: interviewee
text: So finally, when I came from the bathroom, she said, Irene, you're beautiful. First time I felt that I am Irene Gutovna again. The mirror told me so.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] something that happened that-- after dinner, she gave me a sweet drink. And I never-- I am not used to a thing like that. And I went right away and fell asleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: An alcoholic drink?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, some sweet drink. I know that I fell asleep. But when I was sleeping, I did have a nightmare. I felt like somebody is with me, kissing me. It was a nightmare. In the morning, she woke me up. She call me, [RUSSIAN], the breakfast is ready. When I come out of bed, I notice I did have, like, scratches on my neck and my breast.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had breakfast. Then, when I came back to the room, Miriam, the young girl, she said, where did you been? I said, well, Dr. Olga invited me. Helga, the other, started laughing. You know, Dummy! What else?

speaker: interviewee
text: And, oh, she gave me new shoes, boots, the doctor, and little coat that was made from men's military coats, you know, Russian-type. I was just so pleased with that. And she told me to-- I was just bragging, showing them. And they told me that-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Helga was really laughing. I could not understand what they wanted. And now, the time come again that I was invited to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Dr. Olga. And she told me that she wanted me to go with her to Moscow. And she told me that she loved me, told me she was a lesbian. I did not know anything what that means or what it is. Only I knew that I wanted normal life. I wanted marry and have children. And so she was not mad at me. But she said that I am your friend. And anytime you want it, you-- It was quite an experience for me, too. And Marushka, that young Russian, she told me that she was a victim also of hers. And now she was leaving because the Russians now were fighting with Finland. And she was leaving with the front. That's the reason she wanted to take me to Moscow. And now, her place-- took Dr. Ksydzof, young Russian who was wounded in the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Olga leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she was leaving. And she wanted me to come for dinner. And she said that she's listening to me, and she will be-- I should not worry or anything. But she did again-- she got me something to drink. And next thing, I woke up, I was in hospital. And Dr. David that worked in the hospital, he came to me. And I ask him what I'm doing there. I was not conscious to Olga brought me to the hospital. And she said that I have a reaction from some medication. And so, Dr. David-- I was crying. And I was very upset. Olga left. Marushka left. And Helga, they left. I was alone now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you crying? What did you think was happening to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I knew that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's the same thing. She used me. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I crying because you cannot trust anyone. I trusted that woman so much, you know. She helped me. So Dr. David tried to help me. And Dr. Ksydzof now took over.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this war with Finland?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1940. I think that was the middle of 1940. Yeah. I know that she left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how that happened? Why did--

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, how could you know? I have my problems in this. And I am not political. I  didn't know. I know only that now it's Ksydzof. And he's next door. I come from 12-hour job. And in his room, there are Russian nurses, laughing and singing. They're drinking. I could not lock my door. But I brought a little tea in a bottle with me. I fell asleep because I was tired. But somebody was in my bed-- awful smell of alcohol. And men was fighting, trying to rape me. When I was fighting with him, my hand drop on the bottle. And, without thinking, I knock him over the head. And he sort of slumped. And I sneaked from underneath-- I was so scared-- and I ran to the hospital. In the hospital, I was so shaken up that Dr. David-- they have a night shift. I could not-- I was not coherent. So he gave me-- just to quiet me down-- some injections. And I fell asleep. When I awaken in the morning, I was so scared. I thought I killed him. All I say to the doctor, I kill him. I kill him. I was so worried.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he sent a nurse to check my room there. Well, my bed was wet. The tea, it was, yes-- But he was not dead. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But then from then on, I became paralyzed with fear because Dr. Ksydzof was just looking at me like a fighter. I was so scared that I started losing weight. And I was getting sick. And Dr. David, I confided in him. And I say, I will die of it. I am just so scared. And so he said that he would try to find a way for me to get out from the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Dr. David Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's-- yes, he spoke Polish. And he could come. He was not a prisoner. He lived in that town, so he was coming and going. But he was a very wonderful man. And he did-- connected with his friend that went to school together, Miriam. And he helped me to get out from there. He bought the ticket. And I was wearing nurse's cap. I was able to sneak out from the hospital. And I did get to the train. And at that train, when I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we went to Svetlana. That is already on the Russian border. It's not Ukraine-- already Russia. And when I went there, a beautiful woman met me. And she said-- was hugging and kissing me so everybody knew. Oh, you are a daughter of my sister. And you come visit me. So she took me to her-- took care of me. Naturally, everybody was supposed to be right away registered. And there was the village man, the commissar. And so she did teach me how to-- she gave me a new name. And she said that I have to, when I go there, she will talk for me. And so she did go with me and registered me by the man and say, she tried to study to be a nurse. And I need the help. We were working with a little dispensary. Now-- that was 1940, towards the-- almost towards winter. It was cold. And I went with her to give injections to schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the danger for a Pole to be on the Russian border at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's not that it was a-- I don't know that that was a danger. I escaped. And Ksydzof in the meantime, was so mad that I escaped that he said I was a German spy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, were you a prisoner in this hospital where you were with Ksydzof?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was brought-- the others were taken as a prisoner of war. And I was taken to-- because I was wounded. So in a way, I did not have where to go or the-- I stayed there. But because he want to rape me, and I escaped. So that's the reason he was really upset.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with your family while this was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I did not even know where my family is or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was going on in Poland at the same time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was now in Russia, on the Russian side. I didn't know anything. And I was not in contact with them, only with Miriam now and Dr. David.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were not in contact with Polish population?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that point. I was in the village. And because that was on the Russian side already. We did have to be very careful because when they would capture, they would send me back. Or they'd make me spy or whatever, especially that Dr. Ksydzof. He was so mad that he did say that I was a spy, and that I was German spy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so that's what he made it. So even Dr. David was so scared that one time he came to visit the friend [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] because he say-- he was bragging that he captured me, and, I mean, yell, and this and that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So now I was working [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] with her in-- there was a outbreak of meningitis, and all kinds of sicknesses. And I was through with her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now, the time came that I overheard-- not I overheard, but Dr. David wrote it to my friend that I am-- that now Russian have a-- they are taking Polish people. And those that wanted to go on this side, go on this side. They go-- he try to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Exchange?

speaker: interviewee
text: Exchange them, yes. And so I wanted to go home. But the danger was now that the Dr. Ksydzof was looking for me. But I wanted so much to go. You know, young people, they're really courageous. And I wanted to see my parents. So finally Miriam gave up. And she let me go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was Miriam living? What village was this, or city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Svetlana. Svetlana.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Svetlana. What did you do there? And how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was there-- I think I went when they-- in the middle of '40. I mean, June or May or-- and I was there till-- now it's Christmastime coming. So it's almost coming there. And there I have found out that I can go. So it was danger for me, but I took that danger. And I wanted go to home. I was arrested now on the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was going-- waiting line. There were big lines of people that wanted go because they escaped from Germans in here, but the family is there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I manipulated because of my name, Irene Gut-- that there was a small, very small, line on one side that the German people from German extraction were. And I spoke some German from high school. And so I said to them-- they let me in early.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got my little paper that my transport was leaving 24 hours from now. Did not have what to do. So I went to the town and tried to go eat. And [INAUDIBLE] of a second, I was in restaurant. And two men from the hospital recognized me. And they call in [INAUDIBLE] And I was arrested. I was taken-- my transport was supposed to leave. But I was taken. And they kept me for about two days and asked me questions. The commissar said that Dr. Ksydzof is in Moscow. But he said that I am a spy. So they asked me where I have gone. And they believed that something was wrong. We were waiting for Dr. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Ksydzof to come back. And on the second night there, I know my transport left. But the commissar thought that I am really spy. So he said, I will let you go. You have a friend. I let you go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't have a friend. But I said, yes, I have a friend. So I was trying to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so he say, where she lives? I say, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was mad. He'd say, you don't look stupid. I say, well, did you not have a-- that you know the people you know this but forgot the name. Well, anyway, he let me go with a Polish [POLISH], you know. He let me go. And when we were coming to that-- I didn't know where I was going. I just prayed, God, give me a sign, give me something. And I was seeing a corner house. And I did see in a fence a little hole, a little board missing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I told him, This is the house. And he wanted to ring the bell. I said, well, nobody will let me in, please. So I shaked his hand, said, thank you, oh, thank you. I know now.

speaker: interviewee
text: He went around the corner. I sneaked through the hole, went through one yard, through another fence, another yard, to another fence. I find myself in a place that I don't know where I am. And only one thing I wanted know-- to get to the railroad station.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I find that somebody went to the outhouse, you know. And I asked the man. He told me where the station is. And I did go there. And there were many people there trying to leave with the bundles. And I was so tired that I just sit down in a corner and fell asleep.
